bins.name: FERC jurisdiction limits on retail
bins.desc: LPPC argues FERC's authority reaches only transmission interconnection, not the terms of retail service, generation cost allocation, or retail wheeling. Asserting jurisdiction over bundled retail transmission would create unintended obligations to regulate retail rates and would conflict with FPA sections 201 and 212(h).
quotes: LPPC asks that this be accomplished while respecting the role reserved under the Federal Power Act ("FPA") to state authorities over retail service, of which large load is an increasing component.

FERC should clarify that if it proceeds with a NOPR, a proposed rule would be limited to the terms of transmission interconnection. The Commission does not have authority to: (1) establish the terms and conditions of retail service to data centers, including the assessment of up-front generation costs to load and the nature of contractual commitments large load may be asked to make; or (2) mandate transmission service to end use customers (retail wheeling).

Further than this, jurisdiction under the FPA for the Commission to control the terms of large load interconnection is far from clear. A declaration that the Commission has jurisdiction over the transmission component of bundled retail sales is a questionable matter, and would have the likely unintended consequence of the Commission taking charge of the transmission cost component of retail sales rates, meaningfully diminishing state-based authority over retail rates.

LPPC asks the Commission to clarify that the ANOPR's disclaimer reserving authority to states encompasses not only rates for direct retail sales but also the ability of Load Serving Entities ("LSEs") to assess the cost of new generation needed to serve large data centers at the outset of service, and to require contractual commitments of specified length to assure cost recovery.

The Commission should expressly hold that its intention is not to preempt the exercise of state authority over the terms of retail service to large load, including the allocation of generation, distribution upgrade costs, retail contract length, and security requirements.

FERC does not have the authority under the FPA to mandate the provision of transmission service to end use customers, a service known as retail wheeling. FPA section 212(h) ("Prohibition on mandatory retail wheeling and sham wholesale transactions") stipulates that "[n]o order issued under this chapter shall be conditioned upon or require the transmission of electric energy: (1) directly to an ultimate consumer; or (2) to, or for the benefit of an entity if such electric energy would be sold by such entity directly to an ultimate consumers...."

This is unnecessary, and inconsistent with planning and operation for retail service, of which large load is a component. The impetus in Order No. 2003 for granting interconnecting generators the opportunity to build out the transmission grid lay in concern that utilities may have a business interest in dragging their feet in providing an interconnection. This is not the case with respect to new retail load, where transmission owning utilities have an obligation to plan, build, and operate facilities under state/local oversight.

bins.name: 20 MW threshold too low
bins.desc: LPPC considers the 20 MW threshold too low and asks FERC to defer to regional variation, noting that ISOs and utilities have already set varied thresholds based on local grid characteristics.
quotes: LPPC asks the Commission to defer to varied regional approaches. Utilities across the country and ISOs/RTOs have set varied thresholds for the definition of large loads, based on judgments regarding grid configuration and available transmission capacity. LPPC is of the view that the 20 MW threshold is low, and that loads substantially larger than 20 MW have been integrated with some frequency under state-based protocols.

bins.name: Joint study may disrupt queues
bins.desc: LPPC supports studying load and generation together where practical but warns that mandatory joint study can disrupt existing processes and delay both generation and load interconnections in some regions.
quotes: While many utilities have found it useful to study new load and generation together, in some settings the introduction of large load into the study process will be disruptive of existing study processes and holds the potential to delay both generation and load interconnections.

bins.name: Deposits and long-term agreements
bins.desc: LPPC supports standardized deposits and withdrawal penalties for large load, but flags that IRS private-use rules on tax-exempt bonds cap public power contract terms at three years, which complicates enforcement and requires coordination with Treasury.
quotes: LPPC asks the Commission to clarify that the ANOPR's disclaimer reserving authority to states encompasses not only rates for direct retail sales but also the ability of Load Serving Entities ("LSEs") to assess the cost of new generation needed to serve large data centers at the outset of service, and to require contractual commitments of specified length to assure cost recovery.

Customer Agreements longer than three years are treated as "private use" under Treas. Reg. § 1.141-7(f)(3), which caps contract terms at three years. Because tax exempt bonds are subject to private business use limits on a system wide and long-term basis, a single large customer contract that fails the current three year test can cause outstanding tax exempt bonds to become taxable private activity bonds.

bins.name: Hybrid facility technical study
bins.desc: LPPC cautions that protection schemes cannot fully eliminate the risk that hybrid facilities exceed net rights under contingencies, and that power electronic load interactions with the grid are inherently location-specific, requiring the transmission owner to retain authority over technical interconnection requirements.
quotes: interconnection of large-load facilities dominated by power electronic loads requires specific attention to potential control interactions with other systems in the grid. The relevance of this issue and the necessary interconnection requirements and restrictions are inherently location-specific based on the strength of the grid and the proximity to potentially interacting systems.

bins.name: Expedited treatment concerns
bins.desc: LPPC accepts that willingness to curtail is a relevant factor but opposes giving it inflexible priority in cluster studies, and insists expedited treatment must never come at the expense of native load interconnections.
quotes: Establishing an inflexible priority for load and resources that agree to curtail within a cluster study can disadvantage other projects and introduce additional uncertainty for costs and timing of needed generation and network upgrades.
Any expedited treatment for curtailable load and hybrid facilities should not come at the expense of interconnecting historical, native load.

bins.name: Full upgrade cost responsibility
bins.desc: LPPC strongly supports assigning 100% of network upgrade costs to large load with no credits or refunds, arguing that credits would shift costs to existing customers who do not benefit from new load as they do from new generation.
quotes: LPPC agrees that load and hybrid facilities should be fully responsible for network upgrades. No credits or refunds should be required, consistent with the principle of assessing costs to cost causers. Large load interconnections of the magnitude contemplated in many parts of the nation hold the potential for substantial cost shifts to existing load.

bins.name: Cost causation, no cost shifts
bins.desc: LPPC consistently insists that large load must bear all incremental costs it causes, including generation investment, distribution upgrades, and network upgrades, and that state regulators must retain authority to enforce this through tariffs and contract terms.
quotes: LPPC asks the Commission to clarify that the ANOPR's disclaimer reserving authority to states encompasses not only rates for direct retail sales but also the ability of Load Serving Entities ("LSEs") to assess the cost of new generation needed to serve large data centers at the outset of service, and to require contractual commitments of specified length to assure cost recovery.

The Commission should expressly hold that its intention is not to preempt the exercise of state authority over the terms of retail service to large load, including the allocation of generation, distribution upgrade costs, retail contract length, and security requirements.

LPPC agrees that load and hybrid facilities should be fully responsible for network upgrades. No credits or refunds should be required, consistent with the principle of assessing costs to cost causers. Large load interconnections of the magnitude contemplated in many parts of the nation hold the potential for substantial cost shifts to existing load.

bins.name: Flexible regional study processes
bins.desc: LPPC supports interconnection studies for large load but urges flexibility to accommodate regional differences, warning that mandatory joint study or inflexible cluster-study priority rules can disadvantage other projects.
quotes: While many utilities have found it useful to study new load and generation together, in some settings the introduction of large load into the study process will be disruptive of existing study processes and holds the potential to delay both generation and load interconnections.

Establishing an inflexible priority for load and resources that agree to curtail within a cluster study can disadvantage other projects and introduce additional uncertainty for costs and timing of needed generation and network upgrades.

interconnection of large-load facilities dominated by power electronic loads requires specific attention to potential control interactions with other systems in the grid. The relevance of this issue and the necessary interconnection requirements and restrictions are inherently location-specific based on the strength of the grid and the proximity to potentially interacting systems.

bins.name: Curtailment flexibility limits
bins.desc: LPPC accepts curtailability as a factor in study sequencing but opposes using it as an inflexible override, and insists any curtailment and dispatch programs for retail-level load remain within state and LSE authority.
quotes: Establishing an inflexible priority for load and resources that agree to curtail within a cluster study can disadvantage other projects and introduce additional uncertainty for costs and timing of needed generation and network upgrades.
Any expedited treatment for curtailable load and hybrid facilities should not come at the expense of interconnecting historical, native load.

bins.name: State retail authority must be preserved
bins.desc: LPPC's central theme is that state regulators have primary authority over retail rates, generation cost recovery, contract terms, and distribution facilities, and that FERC must expressly disclaim any intent to preempt those state roles.
quotes: LPPC asks that this be accomplished while respecting the role reserved under the Federal Power Act ("FPA") to state authorities over retail service, of which large load is an increasing component.

FERC should clarify that if it proceeds with a NOPR, a proposed rule would be limited to the terms of transmission interconnection. The Commission does not have authority to: (1) establish the terms and conditions of retail service to data centers, including the assessment of up-front generation costs to load and the nature of contractual commitments large load may be asked to make; or (2) mandate transmission service to end use customers (retail wheeling).

LPPC asks the Commission to clarify that the ANOPR's disclaimer reserving authority to states encompasses not only rates for direct retail sales but also the ability of Load Serving Entities ("LSEs") to assess the cost of new generation needed to serve large data centers at the outset of service, and to require contractual commitments of specified length to assure cost recovery.

The Commission should expressly hold that its intention is not to preempt the exercise of state authority over the terms of retail service to large load, including the allocation of generation, distribution upgrade costs, retail contract length, and security requirements.

bins.name: No mandatory retail wheeling
bins.desc: LPPC argues FPA section 212(h) bars FERC from ordering retail wheeling, and that any large load interconnection standard that effectively compels retail transmission access to end users exceeds FERC's statutory authority.
quotes: FERC should clarify that if it proceeds with a NOPR, a proposed rule would be limited to the terms of transmission interconnection. The Commission does not have authority to: (1) establish the terms and conditions of retail service to data centers, including the assessment of up-front generation costs to load and the nature of contractual commitments large load may be asked to make; or (2) mandate transmission service to end use customers (retail wheeling).

FERC does not have the authority under the FPA to mandate the provision of transmission service to end use customers, a service known as retail wheeling. FPA section 212(h) ("Prohibition on mandatory retail wheeling and sham wholesale transactions") stipulates that "[n]o order issued under this chapter shall be conditioned upon or require the transmission of electric energy: (1) directly to an ultimate consumer; or (2) to, or for the benefit of an entity if such electric energy would be sold by such entity directly to an ultimate consumers...."

bins.name: Bundled retail transmission jurisdiction uncertain
bins.desc: LPPC warns that claiming FERC jurisdiction over transmission bundled with retail sales would obligate FERC to regulate transmission rates embedded in retail rates, an outcome Order No. 888 deliberately avoided and that courts have not resolved.
quotes: Further than this, jurisdiction under the FPA for the Commission to control the terms of large load interconnection is far from clear. A declaration that the Commission has jurisdiction over the transmission component of bundled retail sales is a questionable matter, and would have the likely unintended consequence of the Commission taking charge of the transmission cost component of retail sales rates, meaningfully diminishing state-based authority over retail rates.

bins.name: Consensus forum over rulemaking
bins.desc: LPPC proposes that FERC convene a multi-stakeholder forum with NARUC, utilities, and cooperatives to develop voluntary large load interconnection protocols, deferring mandatory rulemaking unless voluntary progress stalls.
quotes: LPPC urges the Commission to convene a forum to develop a consensus-based approach to large load interconnection. That forum, overseen by FERC, may include the NARUC and representatives from the Investor Owned Utility, LPPC and cooperative communities.

bins.name: Regional variation in thresholds
bins.desc: LPPC urges FERC to defer to existing regional protocols on size thresholds and study processes, noting that prescriptive national rules could undermine regions that already have functioning large load interconnection procedures.
quotes: LPPC asks the Commission to defer to varied regional approaches. Utilities across the country and ISOs/RTOs have set varied thresholds for the definition of large loads, based on judgments regarding grid configuration and available transmission capacity. LPPC is of the view that the 20 MW threshold is low, and that loads substantially larger than 20 MW have been integrated with some frequency under state-based protocols.

bins.name: IRS bond rules limit public power
bins.desc: LPPC flags that IRS private-use rules on tax-exempt financing cap contract terms at three years, constraining public power utilities' ability to implement long-term large load agreements, and asks DOE to help align Treasury rules with energy policy.
quotes: Customer Agreements longer than three years are treated as "private use" under Treas. Reg. § 1.141-7(f)(3), which caps contract terms at three years. Because tax exempt bonds are subject to private business use limits on a system wide and long-term basis, a single large customer contract that fails the current three year test can cause outstanding tax exempt bonds to become taxable private activity bonds.

bins.name: Native load must not be displaced
bins.desc: LPPC insists that expedited treatment for curtailable large load must not delay interconnection of existing native load, and that new rules must not be applied retroactively to disadvantage historical customers.
quotes: Any expedited treatment for curtailable load and hybrid facilities should not come at the expense of interconnecting historical, native load.

bins.name: No load option-to-build right
bins.desc: LPPC opposes extending the generator option-to-build to load, arguing utilities already have a state-supervised obligation to build for retail load and allowing load to construct transmission facilities intrudes on state jurisdiction.
quotes: This is unnecessary, and inconsistent with planning and operation for retail service, of which large load is a component. The impetus in Order No. 2003 for granting interconnecting generators the opportunity to build out the transmission grid lay in concern that utilities may have a business interest in dragging their feet in providing an interconnection. This is not the case with respect to new retail load, where transmission owning utilities have an obligation to plan, build, and operate facilities under state/local oversight.

bins.name: Power electronics grid stability
bins.desc: LPPC notes that large data center loads dominated by power electronics can cause adverse control interactions with IBR, HVDC, and FACTS devices in ways that are inherently location-specific, requiring transmission owners to retain authority to set site-specific interconnection requirements.
quotes: interconnection of large-load facilities dominated by power electronic loads requires specific attention to potential control interactions with other systems in the grid. The relevance of this issue and the necessary interconnection requirements and restrictions are inherently location-specific based on the strength of the grid and the proximity to potentially interacting systems.
